tweets: Yesterday I bricked a Lightsail box doing what I've done for ten years: adding a second SSH port on Ubuntu 24.04. Locked out. No browser SSH. Instance wouldn't stop. Here is what went wrong and the 2026 recipe that works. #DevOps #Ubuntu #SSH

The recipe I trusted: drop a file in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d with Port 22 and Port 4728. Nudge ssh.socket with an override declaring both ports. Daemon reload, restart, done. Perfect on 22.04. On 24.04 it silently fails. #Ubuntu #sysadmin

Since 22.10 Ubuntu starts sshd via systemd socket activation. ssh.socket binds the port, systemd spawns sshd on connect. Port directives in sshd_config are ignored because sshd never opens the socket itself. First gotcha. #systemd #Linux

Second gotcha. Stock ssh.socket uses explicit entries, not a wildcard:

ListenStream=0.0.0.0:22
ListenStream=[::]:22
BindIPv6Only=ipv6-only

Two specific binds, IPv4 and IPv6 separately. #Ubuntu #systemd

Now add an override with ListenStream=22 and ListenStream=4728. systemd merges overrides with the base unit, it does not replace them. ListenStream=22 is a wildcard claiming IPv4 and IPv6 on 22. Both already bound. Conflict. #Ubuntu #DevOps

ssh.socket enters failed state on next boot. Nothing listens on 22 or 4728. Browser SSH dies too because it needs sshd on 22. Instance is cold gone from the network. This is where I spent several hours. #sysadmin #SSH

Third landmine. Ubuntu 24.04 ships sshd-socket-generator in /usr/lib/systemd/system-generators. On daemon reload it parses sshd_config and writes ListenStream entries into /run. Port lines plus your override becomes a three way merge of collisions. #Linux #systemd

The fix: abandon socket activation. Let sshd bind the ports itself. Mask the generator so it cannot reinject entries. Classic ssh.service, the way it worked for a decade. Code in the next two tweets. #Ubuntu #SSH

sudo tee /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-harden.conf <<'EOF'
Port 22
Port 4728
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
EOF
sudo sshd -t

#Ubuntu #SSH

sudo ln -sf /dev/null /etc/systemd/system-generators/sshd-socket-generator
sudo rm -rf /etc/systemd/system/ssh.socket.d
sudo systemctl disable ssh.socket
sudo systemctl enable ssh.service
sudo reboot

#DevOps #SSH

Two rules I will never break again.

1. Never live restart sshd or ssh.socket over your only session. A failed bind mid restart and you are done. Apply via full reboot.

2. Keep a second SSH session open until the new config verifies.

#DevOps #sysadmin #buildinpublic
